metadata.summary: Interactive product representation before development. Choose fidelity (low/medium/high) by learning needs: concept → paper, flow → clicks, experience → high-fidelity.

content.title: Prototyping
content.summary: Interactive product representation before code. Pick fidelity by learning goal: concept → paper, flow → clickable, experience → high-fi. Plan, build, test, iterate.

why.p: Static designs cannot convey interactions. Usability issues stay hidden until development, when fixing them is expensive. Prototyping surfaces interaction gaps with real users before a single line of production code is written. Empirical anchor: Nielsen Norman Group reports that fixing a defect in production costs roughly 100x more than catching it during the prototyping/testing phase.

list.item: A risky design assumption needs validation before development starts (navigation, onboarding, checkout, auth).
Stakeholders disagree on how a flow should work, not just how it looks.
The design brief contains 3+ open questions about user behaviour the team cannot answer from data.
The team must choose between two or more interaction patterns and needs evidence.
Generating a structured prototype plan and usability test script from a design brief.
Code prototype is needed to validate technical feasibility (animation perf, gesture handling).
The interaction is motion-dependent or too nuanced for text-based planning (agent can plan, not build).
A live coded prototype is required and crosses from UX planning into implementation.
No clear testing hypothesis exists — prototyping without defined learning goals wastes cycles.
Post-launch optimization where A/B testing or analytics provide faster signal at lower cost.
The flow already ships and quantitative product analytics (heatmaps, funnel data) are available.

how-to.step: Read the design brief and extract every assumption phrased as "users will…", "we believe…", "it should be obvious that…". Each becomes a candidate learning objective.
Convert top 1-3 assumptions into testable questions: "Can a first-time user complete X in under N steps without help?"
Apply fidelity decision matrix: concept-level question → paper/wireframe; flow-level → clickable Figma; experience-level → high-fi animated; technical feasibility → code prototype.
Define scope. Include only screens and interactions on the critical path of the question. Excluded surfaces are listed explicitly in the plan under "Not prototyped".
Build the prototype using the chosen tool. Use realistic content — never Lorem Ipsum.
Write 3-5 task scenarios in neutral voice. Avoid leading verbs ("easy", "simple", "quickly").
Recruit 5 representative participants per audience segment (Nielsen: 5 users find ~85% of usability issues).
Run sessions. Observe silently. Capture quotes verbatim. Note time-on-task, completion, errors, hesitations.
Synthesize into ranked issue list (severity 1-3 by impact). Severity 1 = blocks task; Severity 2 = degrades but completable; Severity 3 = polish.
Decide ship / iterate / kill with PM and designer. Document the diff: pre-test plan vs. post-test findings.

rule.statement: Choose fidelity based on what you need to learn, not on how much time you have.
rule.rationale: Fidelity that exceeds learning needs wastes design time and creates false attachment to visuals before validation.

list.item: Low (paper, basic wireframes) → concept testing, hours to create. Cost: low. Risk of bias: low.
Medium (clickable screens, key interactions, Figma hotspots) → flow testing, days to create. Cost: medium. Best for navigation/IA validation.
High (near-final, animated, ProtoPie/Framer) → experience testing, weeks to create. Cost: high. Use only when motion or polish is the variable under test.
Code (HTML/CSS/React, Storybook) → technical feasibility, engineering time. Cost: highest. Only when production constraints are the question.

rule.statement: Write learning objectives before selecting tools or fidelity level.
rule.rationale: Objectives determine scope; scope determines fidelity; fidelity determines tool. Reversing this order produces over-built prototypes.

list.item: Step 1 — Define objectives: each as a testable question (e.g., "Can users complete checkout without help in ≤90 seconds?"). Limit to 1-3 per round.
Step 2 — Determine fidelity: apply matrix above. When in doubt, start lower; you can always raise fidelity in iteration N+1.
Step 3 — Create prototype: include key screens, critical interactions, realistic content, and a clear user path. Prototype the riskiest assumption first.
Step 4 — Test with users: define 3-5 tasks, observe without intervening, note failures and confusion. Record sessions if consent obtained.
Step 5 — Iterate: fix Severity 1 issues, re-test if critical flows changed, document decisions, hand off.

rule.statement: Limit moderated test tasks to 3-5 per session.
rule.rationale: More tasks produce fatigue and diluted observations, not more insight.

list.item: Paper — sketches and paper screens; fast, cheap, anyone can facilitate. Best for early divergent concept testing.
Clickable (Figma hotspots) — linked screens; quick to build, shareable, testable remotely via prototype URL.
Interactive (Figma/Framer/ProtoPie) — animations and transitions; realistic but time-intensive. Use when motion is the variable.
Code (React/HTML) — technical validation; highest fidelity, real implementation constraints. Pair with Storybook for component-level prototypes.

example.title: Clickable Figma prototype for checkout flow validation
example.p: Create screen designs, add hotspots, link screens, define transitions. Tests flow comprehension without investing in animations.

example.title: Building a ProtoPie interactive prototype with full animations before any user has seen the basic flow
example.p: Wastes days on polish that testing may invalidate entirely.

list.item: Prototype link (interactive reference, view-only).
Screen specs (measurements, spacing tokens, breakpoint behavior).
Interaction specs (transition timing, easing, gesture requirements).
Assets (icons, images, fonts) named per project naming convention.
Component library reference (Figma library link or Storybook URL).
Edge cases documented (error states, empty states, loading states, offline).
"Not prototyped — why" section listing excluded surfaces.

rule.statement: Document what was NOT prototyped and why.
rule.rationale: Prevents stakeholder scope creep during handoff; sets correct expectations for what remains unvalidated.

rule.statement: Prototype only the flows that answer your stated learning objectives.
rule.rationale: Prototyping everything wastes time and makes tests too long. Focused scope produces actionable findings.

rule.statement: Use realistic content — real product names and plausible data — not Lorem Ipsum.
rule.rationale: Placeholder text invalidates navigation and reading behavior; users cannot evaluate information hierarchy with dummy content.

antipattern.description: Too high fidelity too early — polishing visuals before flow validation.
antipattern.reason: Wastes design time and creates emotional attachment to visuals that testing may invalidate.

antipattern.description: Building a prototype but not testing it with users.
antipattern.reason: The artifact has no value without learning; testing is the point.

antipattern.description: Ignoring test findings — iterating the design without addressing discovered issues.
antipattern.reason: Testing without acting on results is a waste of participant time and produces no product improvement.

antipattern.description: No clear hypothesis — prototyping without defined questions.
antipattern.reason: Without a hypothesis, findings cannot be evaluated and the next iteration has no direction.

antipattern.description: Leading questions in test script ("Was that easy?", "Did you find it intuitive?").
antipattern.reason: Suggestive phrasing skews answers toward positive bias; neutral phrasing ("describe what you just did") produces valid data.

rule.statement: Agent-generated test scripts must be reviewed for leading questions before use with participants.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to suggestive phrasing ("did you find it easy to..."); neutral questions are required for valid qualitative data.

rule.statement: A single participant who cannot complete a critical task is a severity-1 finding, regardless of sample size.
rule.rationale: Minority findings must not be averaged away; severity is determined by impact, not frequency alone.

rule.statement: The agent must not decide unilaterally to proceed to development; that decision belongs to the designer and PM after reviewing synthesis.
rule.rationale: Agent synthesis is advisory; the ship/hold decision requires human judgment on risk appetite and product context.

rule.statement: Code prototypes written by an agent are not production code; do not commit to main or use as implementation basis without full engineering review.
rule.rationale: Agent HTML/CSS prototypes miss micro-interaction nuance and may use patterns incompatible with the production stack.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Workflow: a Claude subagent receives a feature brief (user goal, technical constraints, open design questions) and outputs a Prototype Plan document with objectives, fidelity rationale, scope (included/excluded flows), interactive element table, and a usability test script with neutral tasks. After human-run testing, a second agent pass synthesizes raw session notes into a ranked issue list with severity ratings and recommended design changes. The agent never decides ship/hold — it produces evidence; humans decide.
section.prompt:
You are a UX prototyping assistant. Read the design brief below and produce a Prototype Plan in markdown with these sections, in order:
1. Learning Objectives (1-3 testable questions, neutral phrasing) 2. Fidelity Decision (low | medium | high | code) + one-paragraph rationale 3. Scope — Included Flows (bullet list of screens/interactions) 4. Scope — Excluded ("Not prototyped — why") (bullet list) 5. Interactive Elements Table (columns: Element | Trigger | Behavior | State Variants) 6. Test Script — 3-5 task scenarios in neutral voice (no "easy", "simple", "quickly", "obvious") 7. Success Metrics per Task (completion %, time-on-task target, error count) 8. Open Risks (assumptions still unvalidated after this round)
Constraints: - Realistic placeholder content only (no Lorem Ipsum). - Tasks must be neutral: "Sign up for an account" not "Easily sign up". - If the brief lacks information, list questions in an "Inputs Required" appendix instead of guessing.
Brief: <<<BRIEF>>>

You are a UX research synthesis assistant. Input: raw notes from N usability sessions. Output a markdown report with:
1. Executive summary (3 bullets: what worked, what failed, recommendation). 2. Ranked Issues table (columns: ID | Severity 1-3 | Description | Frequency n/N | Verbatim quote | Suggested fix). 3. Severity rules: 1 = blocks task; 2 = completable but degrades trust/efficiency; 3 = polish. 4. A single participant blocked on a critical task = severity 1 regardless of n. 5. Final section "Decision Inputs" — list pros/cons for ship vs. iterate. Do NOT make the decision.
Notes: <<<NOTES>>>

list.item: Playwright — automate interaction with HTML/code prototypes for smoke-test validation. Install: `npm install -D playwright && npx playwright install`. Docs: https://playwright.dev
Puppeteer — screenshot HTML prototypes at multiple viewport sizes. Install: `npm install puppeteer`. Docs: https://pptr.dev
http-server — serve static HTML prototype locally without build step. Install: `npm install -g http-server`. Repo: https://github.com/http-party/http-server
Storybook — component-level interactive prototyping in code (React, Angular, Vue). Init: `npx storybook@latest init`. Docs: https://storybook.js.org
scaffold-prototype.sh (this methodology) — generate a minimal HTML click-through skeleton in seconds.

commands.command: npx storybook@latest init && npm run storybook

list.item: Always write learning objectives before choosing fidelity — fidelity follows what you need to learn, not timeline pressure.
Prototype the riskiest assumption in the design, not the easiest-to-build flow.
Use realistic content (real product names, plausible data) — placeholder text invalidates navigation and reading behavior tests.
Limit moderated test tasks to 3-5 per session; more tasks produce fatigue, not more insight.
Document "what we did NOT prototype and why" — this prevents stakeholder scope creep during handoff.
After testing, produce a diff of the pre-test prototype plan vs. post-test findings before starting iteration.
Recruit 5 users per segment (Nielsen). Add 5 more only if the audience splits into distinct personas.

checklist.check: Learning objectives written as 1-3 testable questions before any tool is opened.
Fidelity matched to question type via decision matrix.
Scope explicitly lists excluded screens with reasons.
All copy uses realistic content; zero Lorem Ipsum strings present.
Test script has 3-5 tasks, all phrased in neutral voice.
Edge cases documented (error, empty, loading, offline).
Recordings consent collected before sessions begin.
Ranked issue list produced with severity 1-3 ratings.
"Not prototyped — why" section present in handoff artifacts.
Pre-test vs. post-test diff committed alongside iteration plan.

verify.step: Run `bash templates/scaffold-prototype.sh demo 3` and confirm directory `prototype-demo/` with `screen1.html`-`screen3.html` is created and screens link forward/back.
Serve with `npx http-server prototype-demo/ -p 8080 -c-1` and open http://localhost:8080/screen1.html — verify navigation works in browser.
Open `templates/prototype-plan.md` and confirm all sections from the agentic-workflow prompt are present (objectives, fidelity, scope, elements, script, metrics, risks).
Lint the test script: grep -iE "easy|simple|obvious|intuitive|quickly" templates/testing-notes.md should return 0 occurrences inside task statements.
Validate methodology XML: `python3 scripts/validate-methodology-xml.py skills/faion/knowledge/solo/ux/ux-ui-designer/prototyping/methodology.xml` must print PASS.
Success criterion for the prototyping round: at least one Severity-1 finding has a documented fix proposal OR all critical tasks have ≥80% completion across participants.

template.purpose: Prototype plan: objectives, fidelity, scope, interactive elements, test script
Per-session observation log: tasks, quotes, issues, recommendations
Bash script to scaffold a minimal HTML click-through prototype
